evidence_from_plot: Chow and Su spend time reenacting how their spouses' affair may have begun, and continue recreating scenes from the affair rather than confronting the real people or moving forward. They idealize their relationship and each other by avoiding the same 'mistake' their spouses made, while their own connection remains trapped in rehearsal and imagination rather than reality.
film: In the Mood for Love / 花樣年華

stimulus:
Some people protect themselves
by making strangers into something safer than they are.

A small gesture becomes proof of goodness.
A quiet person becomes someone who must be lonely, like you.

When you meet someone new,
do you tend to see them as they show themselves,
or fill in the rest with what you need them to be?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Amélie projects her own loneliness onto the girl in Renoir's painting during conversations with Dufayel, who recognizes this pattern. She idealizes Nino and others around her, creating elaborate narratives about their inner lives while avoiding direct confrontation with her own isolation.
film: Amélie / Le Fabuleux destin d'Amélie Poulain

stimulus:
There are people you've hurt.
There are people who've hurt you.

But sometimes,
when you think back,
you only pull up the warmth.

The early days. The good parts.
The version of them you wished had stayed.

When a relationship ends,
do you tend to remember what went wrong,
or what once felt right?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Joel re-experiences his memories of Clementine in reverse chronological order as they are erased, starting with their last fight. As he reaches earlier, happier memories, he realizes that he does not want to forget her.
film: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

stimulus:
Sometimes you remember a person
not as they were in the hard moments,
but in the early light—
when everything felt possible.

When a relationship ends,
do you tend to sort through what went wrong,
or replay the best parts first?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The framing device of the film: Alvy Singer is trying to understand why his relationship with Annie Hall ended a year earlier. Rather than simply moving on, he revisits their relationship, particularly the good moments—cooking lobster together, their first date, the balcony scene with wine. This reflects idealization: emphasizing the positive memories and the 'different' quality of this relationship compared to his previous marriages, even as he tries to understand its failure.

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone for a short time,
and they feel more real than people you've known for years.

Even when it's over,
part of you wants to keep them perfect in memory.
Not what went wrong. Not what was complicated.
Just how it felt when it was good.

When something ends before it breaks,
do you tend to remember it clearly,
or hold it a little higher than it was?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Elio's intense summer relationship with Oliver that lasted only weeks but became transformative. The relationship ended while still passionate (Oliver's departure), before complications or decay. Elio's father acknowledges the rarity and beauty of what they had. The film's nostalgic structure presents the memory as elevated and timeless.

stimulus:
Sometimes someone who's hurt you
still looks perfect from a distance.

Not because they've changed,
but because the feeling needs somewhere to go.

When someone has disappointed you deeply,
do you tend to see them more clearly,
or find yourself remembering them better than they were?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Detective Hae-jun becomes infatuated with Seo-rae despite (or because of) her being a murder suspect. Even after discovering she murdered her husband and manipulated him, destroying his professional pride, he covers up the evidence for her. He continues to be drawn to her thirteen months later, taking on her second husband's murder case. This shows idealization—he maintains an elevated, romanticized view of her despite clear evidence of her destructive nature and the harm she caused him.

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone
right when you learn something can't last.

You could let them see it all—
the mess, the limit, the truth.
Or you could step back,
let them remember you intact.

When something good arrives at the wrong time,
do you try to hold it anyway,
or let it go before it breaks?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jung-won finds out he has a terminal disease just as he develops feelings for Da-rim. Instead of continuing the relationship, he breaks off all contact by closing his photo shop, wanting her to remember him happily rather than watching him deteriorate. He later observes from a distance that she's doing well again, satisfied that protecting her from his fate was the right choice.

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone new
and they remind you of someone you lost.

The way they laugh. The way they turn their head.
Maybe nothing concrete—just a feeling.

When that happens,
do you tend to pull toward them,
or step back and protect the memory?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: In-woo becomes infatuated with his student Hyun-bin because he notices similarities between him and his deceased first love Tae-hee. He idealizes Tae-hee's memory and projects it onto Hyun-bin, making phone calls, finding excuses to touch him, unable to let go of the perfect image of his lost love. This idealization drives him to pursue the student despite the consequences to his marriage, career, and social standing.
film: Bungee Jumping of Their Own / 번지점프를 하다

stimulus:
Some people build empires to prove something.
Not just success—proof.

They collect power, attention, loyalty.
They shape the room, the story, the memory.

But sometimes the bigger it gets,
the more it feels like evidence of something missing.

When you achieve something,
do you feel satisfied,
or like you need to achieve more to feel real?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Kane's relentless acquisition of wealth, newspapers, and influence despite already being one of the richest men in the world; his manipulation of public opinion, his political ambitions, and his construction of the vast Xanadu estate—all suggesting an attempt to assert omnipotent control over his world, yet dying alone with only a childhood memory ('Rosebud'), indicating the defensive grandiosity masked deep vulnerability and loss from being separated from his mother.

stimulus:
Some people feel most alive
when they're controlling something large.

The market. The room. The outcome.

It can feel like proof—
that you're not small, not powerless.

When you've won something,
do you feel like you've become more,
or like you've just forgotten less?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The protagonist is described as 'ruthless' and 'corners the market in copper'—exerting total control over a financial system. This reflects omnipotence: the defense mechanism where one inflates their sense of power to avoid feelings of helplessness or vulnerability. The fact that he 'ultimately ruins the finances of himself and his friends' suggests the unsustainability of this inflated self-image.

stimulus:
Some people rise by learning the limits.
Others rise by believing there aren't any.

When you want something badly,
do you tend to study the system and wait for an opening,
or push through like the rules don't apply to you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Tony Montana's rapid rise from dishwasher to drug lord by repeatedly overstepping boundaries—negotiating unauthorized deals with Sosa behind Frank's back, taking over Frank's operation, believing he can operate above the law, and dismissing warnings until his final downfall. His omnipotence defense is shown through his refusal to accept limits: 'I never trusted Omar,' his confrontation with Frank, his belief he can manage a vast empire while intoxicated, and his ultimate inability to see consequences until too late.

stimulus:
Some people build something large
and feel like they've earned the right to be untouchable.

Success becomes proof.
Control becomes identity.

When you've won enough times,
do you tend to believe the rules still apply to you,
or that you've moved past them?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Daniel Plainview's ruthless quest for wealth, his increasing isolation and alcoholism despite massive success, his violent actions (beating Eli, murdering the imposter Henry) without consequence, and his final state as extremely wealthy but alone in a mansion - demonstrating how accumulated power and success led him to believe he was above normal human limitations and moral constraints.

stimulus:
Sometimes when things fall apart,
there's a voice that says:
I can still do this. I can make it real through will alone.

Even when the ground has shifted,
even when others have stopped believing—

do you tend to let go and reassess,
or push harder into the vision you started with?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Aguirre's escalating delusion of control and grandeur as the expedition deteriorates—leading a mutiny, proclaiming a new empire, continuing downriver despite starvation, death, and obvious failure. He maintains his belief in El Dorado and his own power even as everything collapses around him. The omnipotence defense is shown through his refusal to acknowledge reality and his insistence that sheer will can override circumstances.
film: Aguirre, the Wrath of God / Aguirre, der Zorn Gottes

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone
who stopped following the rules everyone else obeys.

And from where they stand,
it looks like they've found something truer.
Like they've become untouchable.

When you see that,
do you feel drawn to it,
or does it scare you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Colonel Kurtz operating outside military command, waging his own brutal war without permission, commanding troops who worship him as a god-like figure at his remote outpost. This represents the omnipotence defense mechanism—believing oneself to be all-powerful, above rules and human limitations.

stimulus:
When you've lost something that mattered,
and people stop seeing you the way they used to,

there's a choice:
accept the smaller space you're given,
or find a way to take the stage back.

When your position shrinks,
do you tend to step back quietly,
or push to reclaim it?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Abhijit Ganguly is a film director who has long lost his fame and is recognized by no one. When he learns of his terminal disease, instead of accepting his diminished status, he decides to make a comeback film. After being betrayed and left ruined, he creates a reality TV show to expose those who wronged him and reclaim his position - demonstrating omnipotence as a defense mechanism against powerlessness and irrelevance.

stimulus:
Some people are drawn to power
not because they want it,
but because they want to be near it.

It can feel like being chosen.
Like you've become necessary.

When someone powerful takes interest in you,
do you tend to step back and ask why,
or do you step closer, feeling seen?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Garrigan's fascination with and mesmerization by Amin's charm and charisma, remaining in his employment despite knowing better, drawn complicitly into corruption through the seduction of being close to power and feeling like a confidant

stimulus:
When everything around you is collapsing,
there's a way of speaking that refuses to admit it.

Giving orders as if they'll still be followed.
Planning for a future that isn't coming.

In moments when you've clearly lost control,
do you tend to acknowledge it,
or keep issuing commands?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Hitler's continued insistence on counter-attacks and military orders despite being told the war is lost; his refusal to leave Berlin or surrender; ordering executions and promotions even as the Reich crumbles; forbidding retreats and expecting obedience from armies that no longer exist. The omnipotence defense is shown through his persistent belief that his orders still carry absolute power even when reality contradicts this.

stimulus:
Some people believe the world rewards compromise.
Others believe compromise is exactly what weakens you.

When you're pressured to adjust,
to fit in or be more practical,

do you tend to bend a little,
or does something in you go rigid?

What does that rigidity protect?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Howard Roark's refusal to compromise with architectural establishment, his expulsion for not adhering to convention, being fired for refusing classical style, and his overall intransigence against conformity despite financial struggle - embodying omnipotence defense mechanism through absolute conviction in his own vision and standards

stimulus:
Some people feel strongest
when they're proving something.

When the stakes are high,
when everyone's watching,
when winning feels like the only proof that matters.

But sometimes winning becomes the thing itself—
not what you're protecting, just that you can.

When you're good at something,
do you feel like you're using a skill,
or like the skill is using you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Kevin's entire arc: he has never lost a case, proceeds to defend guilty clients (Gettys, Cullen) despite knowing the truth, neglects his wife and her suffering because winning the case feels more important. Milton points out that Kevin made all these choices himself—he 'set the stage' but Kevin chose to win 'regardless of the cost.' The omnipotence defense mechanism is Kevin's belief that his talent and winning streak make him invincible and justify any moral compromise.

stimulus:
Some people wait for the right moment.
Others decide they've waited long enough.

When the system feels rigged,
do you keep your head down and stay inside it,
or
do you find a way to break the rules?

What makes you choose one over the other?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The plot centers on three employees at a secure money depot who go from being rule-following workers (John as 'seemingly straightlaced manager,' Marcus moving cash trolleys, Chris as security guard) to orchestrating a brutal armed robbery. The defense mechanism of omnipotence involves feeling above ordinary rules and limitations, believing you have the power to transcend normal constraints—which aligns with these characters' shift from compliance to criminal control.

stimulus:
Sometimes people listen to you
not because they care,
but because they need something.

And the moment you become inconvenient,
the same attention turns into distance.

When someone gets close and wants to know everything,
do you tend to open up,
or keep the center to yourself?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Byung-doo reveals his darkest secrets (the murders) to his friend Min-ho when seeking consolation. Min-ho was interviewing him for film material, appearing as a friend but ultimately using the information. This leads to Min-ho going to the police and Byung-doo's downfall. The friendship that seemed genuine was actually extractive and became dangerous when Byung-doo became inconvenient.

stimulus:
There are some things
you can't undo by accepting them.

So staying numb,
staying distant,
keeps them unreal.

When something unbearable has happened,
do you let yourself feel it,
or do you keep moving like it hasn't?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Lee's refusal to accept responsibility as Patrick's guardian because it requires returning to Manchester; his deflection of Randi's apology saying he doesn't deserve it; his general depressed and asocial behavior as a janitor living alone, avoiding connection - all show his denial of processing the unbearable trauma of his children's death. He stays numb and distant rather than facing the grief directly.

stimulus:
After something breaks,
some people want to talk about what happened.
Others want to act like it never did.

As if returning to routine
might restore what was there before.

When loss arrives,
do you tend to face it directly,
or try to keep things looking normal?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Beth denies her loss and is determined to maintain the appearance of perfection and normality after Buck's death. She refuses to visit Conrad in the hospital, avoids emotional confrontation, and hopes to restore her family to what it once was by maintaining composure rather than processing grief.

stimulus:
When someone you love is gone,
there are people who say:
"They're still here. I can feel them."

And part of you knows
it might not be real.

But if someone offered you a way to hear them again—
even through someone else's voice—
would you say yes,
or would you walk away?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Vasil refuses to accept his wife's death and seeks out a psychic to contact her, despite it likely being fraud. He stubbornly insists on doing things his own way even when his son tries to bring him to his senses. This is denial - refusing to accept the reality of loss by believing in the possibility of communication with the dead.

stimulus:
When something is falling apart,
there's a version of yourself that knows.

But there's also a version that keeps going,
that treats the decision as still unmade.

When you've already chosen something,
do you admit it right away,
or do you keep pretending the choice is still open?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Ben has already decided to drink himself to death in Las Vegas, but continues his relationship with Sera as if he might change course. He refuses rehab, maintains his drinking, yet acts as though the relationship could continue. This is denial: knowing the outcome while behaving as if it's still undecided.

stimulus:
There are moments when the people around you
say something is a problem.

But it doesn't feel like one yet.
Not in the way they mean.

So you say you forgot,
or that it was just this once,
or that you'll handle it later.

When someone points something out about you,
do you tend to take it in right away,
or
find a way to soften it first?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Don claims to have forgotten about the bottle hidden outside the window when Wick discovers it. Throughout the film, he repeatedly denies the severity of his drinking problem, makes excuses (like saying he'll write a novel about it instead of addressing it), and avoids confronting what others see clearly—that he needs help.

stimulus:
Sometimes the body starts forgetting
before the mind is ready to admit it.

A word slips.
A street looks unfamiliar.

You can call it tiredness,
stress,
just one of those days.

When something small goes wrong with you,
do you name it right away,
or explain it away a little longer?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Alice forgets a word during a lecture and becomes lost during a jog on campus, but these early symptoms are initially minimized before she receives her diagnosis. The denial defense mechanism is central to the early stage of her Alzheimer's journey, where symptoms appear but their significance is not immediately acknowledged.

stimulus:
There's a difference between
having a reason to keep going
and refusing to see what's already happening.

Sometimes hope starts to look like pretending.
Sometimes energy starts to look like panic.

When someone close to you says they're worried,
do you take it in,
or do you hold tighter to the thing that's keeping you up?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Sara's refusal to acknowledge her amphetamine addiction despite Harry's concern. She insists the TV show invitation and her friends' admiration are her reasons to live, even as her behavior becomes increasingly erratic and dangerous. She denies the harm of the pills, increasing her dosage while her reality distorts.

stimulus:
There are people who prepare for bad news.
And people who refuse it, even when it's already arrived.

When something feels final,
do you tend to face it early,
or keep acting like there's still time?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The film depicts a planet called Melancholia on a collision course with Earth. The main character Justine accepts the inevitability of the apocalypse, while her sister Claire denies it and clings to hope that the planet will pass by. This creates the core tension between denial (Claire's response) and acceptance (Justine's response) when facing an undeniable, approaching end.

stimulus:
There are things we can't fix.
But we can still stay.

When someone you love begins to fade,
do you prepare yourself for what's coming,
or do you refuse to let the thought in?

Do you rehearse the loss,
or act like nothing has shifted?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The 2012 Haneke film 'Amour' centers on an elderly couple dealing with the wife's deteriorating health after a stroke. The husband cares for her as her condition worsens, refusing outside help and institutional care—an act often interpreted as denial of the inevitable progression and his inability to accept her loss of autonomy and approaching death.

stimulus:
Sometimes you know something that would hurt the people you love.
If you say it, they suffer.
If you hide it, you carry it alone.

In moments like that,
do you tend to speak it clearly,
or
shape the truth into something easier?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Nader lies to the court about whether he knew of Razieh's pregnancy. He admits to Termeh that he lied 'for her sake and his father's' because he cannot go to prison. Termeh then also lies to protect her father, telling the judge he didn't know about the pregnancy. Both father and daughter deny the truth to protect each other and their family unit.

stimulus:
Sometimes you remember doing something
that hurt someone else.

Not recently—
years ago, when you were young.

When that memory surfaces,
do you tend to bring it up and face it,
or let it stay quiet
and hope it stays in the past?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Georges' childhood actions against Majid (lying about him being sick, making him kill the rooster, getting him sent away) and his consistent refusal to acknowledge or take responsibility for these actions throughout the film, even when directly confronted with video evidence and Majid's suicide. He explains what happened to Anne only after extreme pressure, and even then shows resistance to fully accepting guilt.

stimulus:
Sometimes people decide what happened
before they ask you what happened.

You can say it didn't.
You can repeat yourself.
But the story has already hardened around you.

In moments like that,
do you keep saying the truth out loud,
or do you stop trying to correct it?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Grethe believes children don't lie about such things and alerts authorities based on Klara's fabricated story. When Klara later contradicts herself, the adults interpret this as denial of her ordeal rather than considering she may have lied initially. Lucas is shunned despite his innocence, and the community has already decided his guilt regardless of his protests or the lack of evidence (like the nonexistent basement).

stimulus:
There are things we sometimes choose
not to let be real yet.

Not forever—
just not today.

When something difficult sits in front of you,
do you tend to name it out loud,
or keep moving as if it hasn't landed?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Raimunda hides Paco's corpse and claims he has 'run off' rather than immediately confronting what happened. She continues working at the restaurant, earning money, and carrying on with daily life while concealing the truth. The entire family operates around unspoken truths: Irene's survival, Paula's parentage, the sexual abuse. Characters actively avoid acknowledging painful realities even when confronted with evidence (Raimunda fears Agustina has dementia rather than accept her mother's ghost exists).

stimulus:
There are moments when something feels wrong inside,
but naming it makes it real.

So instead of saying it,
you keep moving.
You act normal. You insist you're fine.

When you feel exposed or small,
do you tend to admit what you're feeling,
or
bury it and carry on?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The plot explains that shame involves painful self-evaluation and feelings of worthlessness, and that 'there is an intrinsic connection between shame and the mechanism of denial.' It notes that 'the person experiencing shame might not be able to, or perhaps simply will not, identify their emotional state as shame.' This reflects denial as refusing to acknowledge the emotion.

stimulus:
Sometimes the hardest person to forgive
is the one who says they don't need it anymore.

They found their own peace.
They moved on without you.

When someone who hurt you
seems already free,
do you let that be enough,
or does it feel like something was taken twice?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Shin-ae visits the prison to forgive Do-seop (the man who killed her son), but discovers he has already found God and been forgiven. She cannot accept that God forgave him before she could—denying her the agency and closure she sought. This triggers her breakdown, as she refuses to accept this reality.

stimulus:
There are people we protect
even when the truth is already visible.

Not because we don't see it,
but because seeing it means everything changes.

When someone you love is clearly in the wrong,
do you name it right away,
or do you wait — hoping the moment will pass on its own?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The film title 'Mother' and the focus on maternal relationships suggest a narrative centered on a mother protecting or defending her child, likely involving denial of wrongdoing. The various definitions of motherhood (biological, adoptive, surrogate) and the maternal role of providing care and protection form the basis for a situation where a mother might deny uncomfortable truths about her child to preserve the relationship or her image of them.

stimulus:
There are moments when the past becomes unbearable.

You can trace the line backward—
where things broke, where you broke them.

But sometimes it's easier to keep moving,
to say it was all inevitable,
that nothing could have been different.

When you look back at your own life,
do you let yourself see the turning points,
or do you tell yourself it was always going this way?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Young-ho's final scream 'I want to go back!' while facing the train, combined with the reverse chronology structure showing clear moments where his life deteriorated—from the torture scenes as police, to his affairs and violence, to his rejection of Sun-im's love and the camera. His denial is shown through his continued destructive behavior despite multiple chances to change, and his inability to face these choices until the very end.

stimulus:
Sometimes the people closest to you
see danger in things you're just beginning to discover.

They call it corruption.
You call it becoming yourself.

When someone you love
reacts with fear to something new in you,
do you assume they're seeing something real,
or that they're afraid of their own losing control?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Margaret witnesses evidence of Carrie's telekinetic powers and denounces her as 'a witch corrupted by sin.' This is projection—Margaret's own fear, religious fanaticism, and need for control are projected onto Carrie's emerging abilities. Margaret interprets Carrie's natural development (both her period and powers) as sinful corruption, when really Margaret is projecting her own internal anxieties and rigid worldview onto her daughter.

stimulus:
Sometimes a person hasn't done anything wrong,
but they still bother you.

When they do well, your space feels smaller.
When they're quiet, it feels like they're hiding something.

There are two possibilities:
That person really is unsettling you.
You're already unsettled, and reading it onto them.

In moments like this, which do you tend to believe first?
Do you put your guard up,
or hold off on your reaction for a moment?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Nina's relationship with Lily demonstrates projection throughout the film. Nina becomes convinced that Lily intends to replace her and views Lily's actions as threatening, even when Lily hasn't necessarily done anything malicious. Nina projects her own insecurities, competitive anxieties, and internal conflicts onto Lily, seeing her as embodying the uninhibited, sensual qualities Nina struggles with. The hallucinated sexual encounter and Nina's growing paranoia about Lily trying to steal her role are manifestations of Nina projecting her own fears and repressed desires onto Lily rather than acknowledging her internal psychological crisis.

stimulus:
Sometimes the world around you feels rotten,
and you want to clean it up.

But the feeling doesn't always match
what's actually out there.

When you feel disgust toward your surroundings,
do you tend to ask if the disgust comes from outside,
or from somewhere already inside you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Travis becomes disgusted with the crime and urban decay he witnesses and dreams about getting 'the scum off the streets.' His narration reveals his deteriorating mental state and deepening alienation. He projects his internal psychological distress onto the external world, seeing it as corrupt and needing violent cleansing, when the real source of disorder is his own unraveling psyche.

stimulus:
Sometimes the people you're angriest at
aren't really the ones who hurt you.

But they're close. Visible. Easy to name.

When something goes wrong in your life,
do you tend to look for who's responsible,
or pause and ask what made you vulnerable first?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Derek projects his rage and grief over his father's death onto entire racial groups rather than confronting his own pain and vulnerability. He blames others (Black people, Jewish people) for systemic problems and personal loss, refusing to acknowledge that his anger stems from unprocessed trauma. The film shows how he externalizes his internal chaos onto external targets, making them responsible for his suffering rather than dealing with the actual source of his pain.

stimulus:
Sometimes the person you're angriest at
isn't really the one in front of you.

You call them guilty.
You call them reckless, ungrateful, wrong.

But if you stopped and asked yourself—
who are you actually talking to?

When you feel rage toward someone,
do you ask where it's coming from,
or do you let the feeling justify itself?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Juror 3's certainty of the defendant's guilt is driven by his strained relationship with his own son. He projects his anger toward his son onto the defendant, only realizing this at the end when he breaks down and tears up a photograph of himself and his son.

stimulus:
Sometimes the person you're angry at
isn't really the problem.

But it's easier to make them the villain
than to admit you're the one who's been hurt.

When something goes wrong in a relationship,
do you find yourself rewriting what happened,
or do you try to remember it clearly?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Amy projects her own manipulative, vengeful nature onto Nick by fabricating diary entries that paint him as abusive and murderous. She creates an elaborate false narrative where Nick is the dangerous villain, when in reality she is the one planning his destruction. This is classic projection - attributing her own hostile intentions and destructive behavior to him instead.

stimulus:
Sometimes you see someone changing,
and it bothers you more than it should.

Not because they're doing something to you—
but because of what they're becoming.

In those moments,
do you tend to think they're the problem,
or that something in you is reacting?

Do you confront them,
or step back and check yourself first?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Michael suspects others of betrayal and treachery throughout the film—Pentangeli, Roth, even Fredo—while he himself is becoming increasingly ruthless, isolated, and morally compromised. His accusations toward others mirror his own transformation into something darker. This is classic projection: attributing to others the qualities he cannot face in himself.

stimulus:
Sometimes the version of yourself you show the world
starts to feel more real than what's underneath.

You play the part so well
that even you forget where the performance ends.

When you feel a gap between who you are and who you present,
do you tend to think:
the world is forcing you to hide,
or you've been hiding from yourself?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jung's warning about using personas too much and losing one's individuality to their persona; the concept that once a person starts to believe they are their persona, it can have adverse effects; James Hillman's idea that when a person loses their identity to a persona and believes they ARE their persona, they become unstable and unable to act outside their formed persona.

stimulus:
Sometimes the thing that feels wrong in someone else
is actually the thing you haven't let yourself feel.

Their softness looks like weakness.
Their wanting looks like a threat.

When someone's behavior bothers you deeply,
do you tend to see it as their problem,
or pause and ask what it might be reflecting?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Erika projects her own repressed sexuality and emotional disorder onto others throughout the film. She sabotages Anna (cutting her hand with glass) when she perceives Anna as a threat, possibly projecting her own anxieties. She labels Walter as problematic for his interpretation and motivations, when she herself struggles with deep sexual and emotional dysfunction. When Walter calls her 'sick,' she later tries to project normalcy. Her projection defense mechanism prevents her from acknowledging her own psychological disturbance, instead seeing problems in others' behavior, intimacy, or success.

stimulus:
Sometimes you remember yourself as kinder than you were.

A small cruelty from childhood—
maybe it didn't matter.
Maybe someone else would've done the same.

But when the past shows up again,
do you tend to think:
they're the one causing trouble now,
or
you're the one who caused it back then?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Georges projects blame onto Majid for the surveillance tapes, refusing to acknowledge his own childhood cruelty that led to Majid being sent away. He threatens Majid and insists Majid is the perpetrator, rather than recognizing he is being confronted with the consequences of his own past actions. The projection defense allows Georges to avoid facing his guilt.

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone
who seems to see things in you
that you didn't know were visible.

They ask questions that touch something,
and suddenly you're saying more than you meant to.

In moments like that,
do you feel like they drew it out of you,
or that it was already there, waiting?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Mamiya's hypnotic technique involves asking people about their lives and frustrations (as he does with Takabe about Fumie), causing them to project their inner darkness outward into violent acts. Takabe eventually projects his own rage onto Mamiya during interrogation, shouting his resentment about Fumie. The film explores how Mamiya doesn't create the violence—he makes people express what was already inside them. This is projection: the murderers are acting out internal impulses they attribute to external influence.

stimulus:
Sometimes the feelings you can't admit
are the ones you see most clearly in someone else.

When you're suspicious of what someone might do,
is it because of what they've shown you,
or because of something already turning in you?

Do you tend to look outward first,
or pause and check inward?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Iago's manipulation works by projecting suspicions onto Othello - Iago himself is the one who is deceptive, disloyal, and plotting betrayal, yet he convinces Othello to see these qualities in Desdemona and Cassio. Othello projects his own potential for violence and jealousy onto others, believing they are the unfaithful ones when he is the one losing faith. The core mechanism is characters attributing their own unacknowledged impulses (jealousy, deception, disloyalty) onto others.

stimulus:
When someone pushes you too hard,
it's easy to say: they're cruel, they're wrong.

But sometimes you flip it.
Maybe I'm weak. Maybe I can't handle it.
Maybe everyone else would've survived this fine.

When you're hurt by someone's behavior,
do you tend to point outward first,
or turn the blame on yourself?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Fletcher's abusive treatment of Andrew (throwing chairs, slapping, psychological abuse) could trigger projection where Andrew internalizes the abuse and blames himself for not being good enough, rather than recognizing Fletcher's behavior as abusive. This is reinforced by Andrew's excessive practice until his hands bleed, suggesting he believes the problem is his inadequacy rather than Fletcher's cruelty. The defense mechanism of projection here involves Andrew potentially projecting his own harsh self-judgment onto Fletcher's actions, or alternatively, taking Fletcher's abusiveness and redirecting it inward as self-blame.

stimulus:
Sometimes the thing that disturbs you most in someone else
is something you've felt in yourself.

Their cruelty looks like yours.
Their need looks like yours.

When you see something ugly in another person,
do you assume it's just them,
or do you wonder if you're recognizing it?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jeffrey observes Frank's violent, sexually abusive behavior toward Dorothy, yet finds himself entering into a sadomasochistic relationship with her where she encourages him to hit her. He witnesses Frank's brutality but then engages in similar dynamics. The film explores how Jeffrey projects his own darkness onto Frank while unconsciously mirroring the violence he condemns.

stimulus:
When someone accuses you of something you didn't do,
it can feel like the whole world reshapes around it.

Even people who know you
start looking at you differently.

In moments like that,
do you tend to assume others are projecting their own fears onto you,
or do you first question whether you somehow invited the misunderstanding?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The village community projects their anxieties about child abuse onto Lucas despite his innocence. Grethe and the parents believe children never lie about such things, projecting their assumptions rather than examining the truth. The community's collective hysteria reflects their own unexamined fears being placed onto Lucas, while he is left questioning what he could have done differently.

stimulus:
Someone warns you about a person.
Someone else warns you about them.

Both sound sure.
Both sound afraid.

In that confusion,
do you tend to trust the one who arrived first,
or the one who feels more familiar?

Do you look outward for proof,
or inward for what feels true?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jong-goo receives contradictory warnings about who the real demon is—first from Moo-myeong (claiming the stranger is evil), then from Il-gwang (claiming Moo-myeong is the real demon). Unable to determine the truth, Jong-goo projects his own doubt and fear onto Moo-myeong when he sees victim items on her, choosing to believe she is the threat rather than waiting as instructed. This projection leads him to abandon her trap and return home, resulting in his family's death.

stimulus:
Sometimes when a case goes cold,
it's easier to believe someone—anyone—did it.

Even when the details don't fit.
Even when the evidence says otherwise.

Because if they're guilty,
then at least someone is.

When you need an answer badly,
do you look harder at the facts,
or at the person you've already decided on?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Detective Park claims to find suspects by eye contact and forces a confession from Baek Kwang-ho, a mentally handicapped boy, despite lack of evidence. Later they arrest a man masturbating near the crime scene and try to make him fit the profile even though his confession doesn't match the crime details. Park projects his need to solve the case onto convenient suspects rather than following evidence.

stimulus:
Sometimes you know what happened.
But the reason you give for it
isn't quite the truth.

It's cleaner. More defensible.
It protects something—maybe the institution,
maybe your role in it.

When something goes wrong,
do you tend to say what actually happened,
or frame it in a way that holds the structure together?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Colonel Jessep ordered the 'code red' on Santiago but rationalized it as 'training' him for substandard performance and maintaining unit discipline. He justified an illegal, violent act by framing it as necessary for national security and military order. His famous 'You can't handle the truth!' speech reveals how he rationalized his actions as essential duty rather than acknowledging the illegal violence.

stimulus:
There are moments when the risk works out.
You pushed all in, and the table tipped your way.

But winning once doesn't mean
the same move will work again.

When something risky succeeds the first time,
do you tend to see it as proof of your skill,
or as luck you shouldn't lean on?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The ruthless trader corners the market in copper and makes a fortune, but then sells short and ultimately ruins the finances of himself and his friends - suggesting he rationalized his initial success as sustainable skill rather than circumstantial luck

stimulus:
Sometimes you do something you know is wrong,
and at first the guilt is overwhelming.

But then weeks pass.
Then months.
And somehow, you're still here. Life continues.

You start to think:
maybe it wasn't that bad.
Maybe I had no choice.
Maybe it's just… behind me now.

When something heavy happens,
do you tend to keep examining it,
or let time soften it into something you can live with?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Judah has worked through his guilt after arranging Dolores' murder and is enjoying life once more. He tells Cliff that 'with time, any crisis will pass.' He has rationalized his way out of the moral crisis, turning to and then moving past religious guilt, ultimately continuing his life as if the crime can be justified or minimized by the passage of time.

stimulus:
Sometimes you do something you know is wrong,
but you also feel there was no good choice.

And after,
you tell yourself it was necessary.
That you were protecting something important.
That anyone in your position would have done the same.

When you've done something you can't undo,
do you tend to justify it with reasons,
or sit with the feeling that it was simply wrong?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Chris murders Nola and Mrs. Eastby to preserve his affluent lifestyle and marriage. When Nola's apparition confronts him about his crimes, he defends himself saying his actions, though wrong, were committed for a 'grander scheme' and that he can suppress his guilt. This is rationalization - justifying morally wrong actions by reframing them as necessary or serving a greater purpose.

stimulus:
There are jobs where the work itself
isn't hard to defend—
it's defending it out loud that costs something.

When what you do is questioned,
do you tend to justify it
by reframing the terms,
or
by stepping back and reconsidering?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Nick Naylor's entire career is built on rationalizing the indefensible—lobbying for Big Tobacco by reframing cigarette deaths as issues of 'consumer choice' and 'personal responsibility' rather than corporate harm. He doesn't deny smoking is dangerous; he shifts the argument to freedom and consistency (comparing tobacco to Vermont cheese causing cholesterol deaths). His rationalization is so skilled it becomes his identity ('Everyone has a talent... I talk'), allowing him to continue the work without confronting its moral weight directly.

stimulus:
Sometimes you realize too late
that someone was hurting you in ways you couldn't name yet.

And by then, years have passed.
The feelings are still there, but the story has changed.

When that happens,
do you tend to rewrite what it meant back then,
or let it stay complicated?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Michael's relationship with Hanna was physically and emotionally abusive, but as a 15-year-old he couldn't fully comprehend it. The memory 'taints all his other relationships with women' as he struggles throughout his life to come to terms with what happened. He uses rationalization to make sense of the relationship - feeling guilty and believing 'it was something he did' rather than recognizing the problematic power dynamic and abuse. The entire narrative is his adult self attempting to rationalize and make sense of this formative trauma.

stimulus:
There are situations where the right choice
and the survivable choice
don't line up.

When staying honest means you lose everything,
and bending means you keep going—

do you tend to protect the principle,
or protect yourself first?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The firm's leadership chooses to dump toxic assets in a fire sale despite knowing it will destroy market trust and their reputation. They rationalize this as necessary survival ('his desire to avoid the firm's bankruptcy is worth that cost'). Sam Rogers objects morally but complies. The employees are told their work is 'necessary' despite the ethical damage. This is classic rationalization—reframing a self-interested, harmful action as justified or inevitable.

stimulus:
Sometimes you see the pattern early.
Everyone else is moving forward,
but the math doesn't add up.

When you notice something others don't,
do you tend to say it out loud,
or justify staying quiet because no one else sees it yet?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The book follows people who believed the housing bubble was going to burst while the rest of the market continued as if everything was fine. These individuals saw the pattern in the CDO market that others rationalized away or ignored, going against the grain while others justified the status quo.

stimulus:
Sometimes you do something you know is wrong,
but later, when someone calls it out,
you find yourself explaining why it made sense at the time.

Not lying—
just arranging the reasons differently.

When you've done something you regret,
do you tend to sit with the discomfort,
or start building the case for why it happened?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Alex throughout the film never truly takes responsibility for his violent actions. Even after the Ludovico treatment, his 'reform' is purely mechanical—he cannot commit violence because of conditioning, not because he recognizes his actions as morally wrong. The film explores how both Alex and society rationalize their actions: Alex rationalizes his ultra-violence as artistic expression and freedom, while the government rationalizes using torturous conditioning techniques as necessary for political and social order. The prison chaplain's objection that Alex has been 'robbed of his free will' itself becomes a rationalization—focusing on the method rather than confronting the original crimes.

stimulus:
Someone you trust
tells you to do something that feels wrong.

But they have authority.
They sound certain.
And they give you reasons that almost add up.

In that moment,
do you tend to stop and name the feeling,
or keep going and adjust the story later?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The film 'Compliance' (2012) is based on a true incident where a prank caller impersonating a police officer convinces a fast-food manager to detain and abuse an employee. The manager and others comply with increasingly disturbing requests, later rationalizing their actions by citing the caller's authority, their trust in the system, and believing they were following legitimate orders despite clear moral violations.

stimulus:
Sometimes you see something wrong
and you know speaking up will cost you everything.

So you tell yourself:
It's complicated. It's not my place. I'm just one part of a system.

And for a while, that works.

When you're facing something like that,
do you tend to find reasons it's not your problem,
or do you act even when it hurts?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Michael Clayton discovers the U-North coverup memo proving the company knew its weed killer caused deaths. He's offered $10 million and a non-disclosure agreement to stay silent. He initially accepts the money and tries to walk away, rationalizing his complicity by paying off debts and trying to move on. This is classic rationalization - justifying morally questionable inaction with logical-sounding reasons (financial necessity, loyalty to the firm, it's not his case). Arthur Edens also shows the opposite response - he stops rationalizing U-North's actions and breaks from his role as their defender.

stimulus:
Sometimes something gets removed
not because it's bad,
but because someone decided
it no longer serves a purpose.

Later, it gets brought back.
Not because it was missed—
because it became useful again.

When something you valued disappears,
do you tell yourself it wasn't needed,
or do you admit you wanted it to stay?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The letter Z was removed from the Latin alphabet around 300 BC by Appius Claudius Caecus, rationalized as either because 'the appearance while pronouncing it imitated a grinning skull' or because 'the /z/ sound had disappeared from Latin,' making it 'useless.' Later, after the Roman Conquest of Greece, Z was reintroduced to spell Greek words. The removal was rationalized as practical necessity, though the real reason may have been personal distaste.

stimulus:
Sometimes the system you're in
feels broken or unfair.

But you're still part of it.
You still participate.

Do you tend to tell yourself
it's the best option available,
or
do you admit it doesn't sit right with you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The text discusses how elections are used as a democratic tool in modern systems, yet notes they were considered 'oligarchic' in ancient Athens which preferred sortition (random selection). It also mentions electoral reform to improve fairness where systems are lacking. This presents a tension between participating in imperfect systems while recognizing their flaws—a core setup for rationalization as a defense mechanism.

stimulus:
When the real world becomes too harsh to bear,
some people withdraw into a story.

Not to escape forever—
but to survive long enough to keep going.

When life feels unbearable,
do you tend to face it directly,
or build a softer world inside your head?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Ofelia creates/enters a fantasy world with the faun and magical tasks while enduring the brutal reality of her stepfather Vidal's violence, her mother's deteriorating health, and the surrounding war. The fantasy world provides her with meaning, agency, and hope (becoming Princess Moanna) while the real world offers only powerlessness and cruelty. The film deliberately blurs whether the fantasy is real or Ofelia's psychological defense mechanism against trauma.

stimulus:
Some people cope by building a world no one else can enter.

Not a lie exactly.
More like a place where the rules are different,
where you're not powerless.

When life gets hard,
do you tend to stay and face it directly,
or retreat into something only you control?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The core premise of Bridge to Terabithia involves children creating an imaginary fantasy kingdom (Terabithia) as an escape from difficult realities in their lives - bullying, family stress, feeling like outsiders. This imaginary world serves as an autistic fantasy defense mechanism where they have control and power they lack in real life.

stimulus:
Sometimes the distance between you and others
feels safer than the risk of real contact.

So you create something in between—
not quite real, not quite pretend.
Just enough to practice with.

When you're afraid of being hurt,
do you tend to reach for something you can control,
or take the risk with something unpredictable?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Lars exhibits avoidance behaviors and haphephobia (fear of touch), causing social isolation. He orders a lifelike doll (Bianca) and treats her as real, creating a safe relationship he can control. Through this fantasy relationship, he eventually becomes able to connect with real people like Margo. The doll serves as a transitional object allowing him to practice intimacy without the unpredictability and risk of real human contact.

stimulus:
Sometimes the world you want
feels more real than the one you're in.

You know the difference.
But crossing back and forth—
it starts to feel optional.

When disappointment builds,
do you tend to adjust the outside,
or retreat into something you can control?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Stéphane's vivid dreams increasingly interfere with his waking life. As reality disappoints him (unfulfilling job, romantic confusion with Stéphanie), the line between dream and reality becomes more blurred. He becomes unsure of what is real, his dreams encroach on waking life, and he retreats into fantasy rather than confronting external reality—culminating in the ambiguous final scene where dream and reality are indistinguishable.

stimulus:
When you were younger,
someone older probably told you something untrue.

Maybe to comfort you.
Maybe because they didn't know either.

And you built a world inside that story—
one that felt safer, or made more sense.

When reality started to contradict it,
did you let the story go,
or did you keep visiting it anyway?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Isabel tells Ana that the monster didn't really die and is like a spirit she can talk to. Ana becomes so absorbed in this fantasy that even after discovering the dead soldier and being found traumatized, she withdraws into herself and continues calling to the spirit by the window, preferring this inner world to engaging with her family or reality.

stimulus:
Sometimes the world you build with someone
feels more real than the one you're standing in.

And when someone threatens to take that person away,
the fantasy doesn't feel like escape anymore—
it starts to feel like the only plan.

When reality becomes unbearable,
do you retreat into imagination,
or force yourself to stay present?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Juliet and Pauline create the fantasy kingdom of Borovnia and the Fourth World, which 'begins to be as real to them as the real world' and 'begins to affect their perception of events in the everyday world.' When faced with separation, 'their fantasy life becomes a useful escape from the real world' and they 'engage in increasingly violent, even murderous, fantasies' culminating in actual murder to prevent separation.

stimulus:
Sometimes the world feels too much,
and you step into another one for a while.

Not to run away—
just to be somewhere you can feel big again.

When things get overwhelming,
do you tend to stay and push through,
or let yourself drift somewhere else first?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Max's bedroom transforms into a jungle environment after being sent to bed, and he sails to an island where he becomes king of the Wild Things—a fantasy world where he has control and power. He eventually returns home to find his supper waiting, suggesting the fantasy served a temporary emotional purpose rather than permanent escape.

stimulus:
Some people respond to loneliness
by reaching outward.

Others build a world inside—
rich, detailed, and just theirs.

When you feel alone,
do you try to connect with what's around you,
or retreat into something you can control?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Amélie copes with her isolated childhood and social disconnection by developing an active imagination and finding contentment in solitary, whimsical pleasures (dipping hand into grain, cracking crème brûlée, skipping stones). Rather than directly addressing her own isolation, she creates elaborate fantasies and secret schemes to help others, maintaining distance from real intimacy even while orchestrating connection for everyone around her.

stimulus:
Some people live more vividly
in the scenes they imagine
than in the ones they're actually in.

When the day feels small or repetitive,
do you tend to stay present in it,
or slip into a version where you're someone else?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Walter Mitty is described as 'an ineffectual person who spends more time in heroic daydreams than paying attention to the real world.' Throughout his mundane shopping trip, he has five heroic fantasy episodes triggered by ordinary details—imagining himself as a Navy pilot, surgeon, assassin, RAF pilot, and facing a firing squad—all while performing routine tasks with his wife.
film: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty

stimulus:
When the world becomes too much,
some people try to find other people.

Others build a smaller world
and live there for a while.

When you're alone with something difficult,
do you try to reach outward,
or inward?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jeliza-Rose spends the summer alone at an isolated farmhouse with only Barbie doll heads for company, embarking on highly imagined and increasingly surreal adventures in the tall grass - she creates an elaborate fantasy world to cope with her isolation and difficult circumstances.

stimulus:
Some people feel like they're watching their life
from the outside.

Like the script was written somewhere else,
and they're just going through the motions.

When life feels unreal,
do you try to snap yourself back into it,
or do you let yourself drift a little longer?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Donnie's psychotherapist Dr. Thurman believes he is 'detached from reality' and experiencing 'daylight hallucinations.' Donnie sleepwalks through much of the film, guided by visions of Frank, suggesting he experiences life in a dissociated, dream-like state rather than being fully present in reality. The 'autistic fantasy' defense mechanism refers to this retreat into an internal, constructed reality when external reality becomes overwhelming.

stimulus:
Sometimes life gets too real to hold,
so you build a version you can control.

A story where you direct every line,
a place where everything has a shape.

When things feel too much,
do you step back into the world,
or do you retreat into something you can design?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Caden creates an increasingly elaborate theatrical production that mimics reality in response to his unraveling life (physical ailments, failed marriage, alienation). He populates it with doppelgängers, lives through constructed versions of his relationships, and eventually takes direction through an earpiece to escape his role as director. This represents autistic fantasy: retreating into an internally controlled world to avoid overwhelming external reality.

stimulus:
Some people have a place they return to in their mind.
A scene that didn't happen, or one that never ended.
It's detailed. Specific. Comforting.

When things get hard,
do you tend to stay in the world as it is,
or slip into one you've built?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Cobb's relationship with Mal - his mind's projection of his late wife that sabotages him, and their fifty years spent in Limbo, a world of infinite subconscious they created together. He cannot let go of this internal world/memory, even though it interferes with his present reality and work.

stimulus:
There are relationships you want to forget.
Not just the end—
the whole shape of it.

But if you could erase someone completely,
would you?

Or would you rather keep the hurt
if it means keeping the early parts too?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Joel decides to erase Clementine after discovering she erased him first. But during the procedure, as he re-experiences their memories in reverse, he reaches the earlier, happier memories and realizes he doesn't want to forget her. He tries to hide her in other memories to preserve them. This captures the core tension: the desire to erase pain vs. the realization that erasing someone means losing the good parts too.
film: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

stimulus:
Some people believe
that if they stay quiet enough, small enough,
they can live inside a life without being caught by it.

When things feel unbearable,
do you tend to speak up and break the shape of it,
or
do you stay so still that maybe the world forgets you're there?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Tae-suk's entire mode of existence—breaking into homes silently, living undetected, and eventually developing extreme stealth in prison to evade detection even from jailers and Min-gyu. Sun-hwa also remains silent throughout their relationship and interrogation. The film's ending emphasizes this fantasy of existing 'outside' reality through invisibility: 'It's hard to tell whether the world we live in is either a reality or a dream.' This reflects autistic fantasy as a defense—withdrawing so completely from harsh reality (abuse, marginalization, social illegibility) that one creates an alternate mode of being that feels protected from the world's violence.

stimulus:
Sometimes the world feels like it's designed to hurt you.
And if you look hard enough,
you can find a pattern that explains it all.

In those moments,
do you tend to organize the pain into a story,
or let it stay scattered and meaningless?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Byeong-gu creates an elaborate alien conspiracy theory to cope with a series of traumas: his mother's poisoning by the pharmaceutical company, his abusive childhood, violent school years, and his lover's death. Rather than face the random cruelty of these events, he organizes them into a coherent narrative where aliens are responsible and he can be the hero who saves Earth. This is the core of his autistic fantasy—transforming unbearable, meaningless suffering into a comprehensible mission.
film: Save the Green Planet! / 지구를 지켜라!

stimulus:
Sometimes the worst place you ended up
isn't actually the problem.

It's small. It's cut off.
But at least no one's asking you for anything.

When life becomes unlivable,
do you try harder to fix it,
or do you let yourself disappear for a while?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Kim Seong-geun is deep in debt and jumps off a bridge, but washes up on Bamseom island. Instead of trying to escape immediately, he begins to like living there—free of his debt and worries of city life. This reflects autistic fantasy: withdrawing into an isolated, self-contained world rather than confronting overwhelming external demands.
film: Castaway on the Moon / 김씨 표류기

stimulus:
Sometimes you feel something about someone,
and you need them to feel it too.

Not just assume they do—
you need them to carry it, embody it, prove it.

When you're uneasy around someone,
do you tend to watch and wait,
or do you push until they show you
what you already suspect is there?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Nina projects her own dark, uninhibited qualities onto Lily, then becomes convinced Lily embodies those threatening traits and is actively trying to replace her. Nina pushes this dynamic until Lily seems to confirm her fears—though much of it exists in Nina's deteriorating mind. This is projective identification: Nina evacuates unwanted parts of herself into Lily, then relates to Lily as if she actually contains those qualities, pressuring the relationship until the projection feels real.

stimulus:
Sometimes the version of yourself you show to others
starts to feel more real than what's underneath.

You play the role so well
that even you forget where the performance ends.

When you feel split between who you are
and who you've been presenting,
do you tend to pull the mask off quickly,
or keep wearing it a little longer?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jung's warning about using personas too much, fearing one might lose their individuality to their persona. The concept that 'once a person starts to believe they are their persona, it can have adverse effects on their personality' and 'once a person loses their identity to a persona... they become unable to act outside their formed persona.'

stimulus:
Sometimes you feel something difficult in someone,
and instead of naming it in yourself,
you try to put it into them.

You push them to feel what you can't hold.
You wait for them to crack, so you don't have to.

When you're overwhelmed by your own feeling,
do you tend to sit with it alone,
or
find someone else to carry it for you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Barbara's loneliness and neediness, which she cannot face in herself, gets projected into Sheba. Barbara orchestrates Sheba's downfall and traps her in dependency, forcing Sheba to enact the desperate isolation Barbara herself feels. Barbara thrives as Sheba disintegrates—she uses Sheba to contain and express her own unbearable emotions.

stimulus:
Sometimes someone treats you cruelly,
and you feel something strange:

You start to believe you deserved it.
You push yourself even harder to prove them right—or wrong.

When someone's harshness gets inside you like that,
do you tend to push them away,
or does part of you start performing for them?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Fletcher's abusive treatment of Neiman causes Neiman to internalize Fletcher's harshness and excessive standards. Rather than rejecting Fletcher, Neiman practices until his hands bleed, breaks up with his girlfriend, and sacrifices everything to meet Fletcher's impossible demands. He absorbs Fletcher's cruelty and makes it his own drive, essentially identifying with the aggressor's projected standards and performing them back. This is projective identification: Fletcher projects his ruthless perfectionism onto Neiman, and Neiman not only receives it but enacts it, becoming the very thing Fletcher demands.

stimulus:
Some people need space to love you.
Others need you smaller.

When someone pulls away the moment you act on your own—
when your presence becomes 'too much' unless you're quiet—

do you shrink yourself to keep the peace,
or do you push back in a way that forces them to feel what you feel?

Even if it's not kind.
Even if it's control dressed as care.

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Alma poisons Reynolds with mushrooms to make him weak and dependent, forcing him to need her care. This is projective identification: she cannot tolerate his emotional distance and dominance, so she induces in him the helplessness and dependency she feels, then 'cares' for him in that state—making him experience what she experiences, while she takes the powerful role she's denied.

stimulus:
There are people who give endlessly,
even when no one asked them to.

They feed the guests. They fix the walls.
They smile through the chaos.

But sometimes what spills out of them
isn't kindness—
it's something they couldn't hold in anymore.

When you feel drained by others,
do you tend to pull back and protect yourself,
or push something outward—anger, a scene, a boundary—
so they finally feel what you've been holding?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Mother continuously tolerates intrusions and chaos from uninvited guests while Him ignores her needs. She cleans, accommodates, and suppresses her frustration until she finally snaps—ordering everyone out, attacking those who ate her child, and ultimately burning down the house. Her rage and destruction are projective identification: she forces others to experience the violation and chaos she has been holding inside throughout the film.

stimulus:
Sometimes you can't say what you want directly,
so you arrange things.

You put someone in a position
where they'll act out what you can't.

Then you wait—
to see if they'll become the feeling you placed in them.

When you want something from someone,
do you ask for it clearly,
or do you set conditions and wait?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Erika gives Walter a letter detailing her masochistic fantasies and provides BDSM paraphernalia, essentially placing her internal violence and desire into him. She arranges the conditions for him to enact her repressed sadistic/violent feelings. Later, Walter does become violent—he attacks and rapes her in the manner she described. She projected her internal fragmented state into him, and he enacted it, though it ultimately overwhelms and destroys their dynamic.

stimulus:
Sometimes you feel something so strongly
that you need someone else to carry it for you.

Not by telling them—
but by making them feel it without knowing why.

When you're full of something you can't hold,
do you try to keep it inside,
or do you pass it along without meaning to?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Carrie's projective identification is seen in how she makes others experience the mockery and rage she feels—especially during the prom massacre. She doesn't just react to being humiliated; she forces the entire room into her internal state of terror and chaos, making them feel what she feels (trapped, attacked, helpless) through telekinetic violence. Her mother Margaret also projects her own shame and 'sinfulness' onto Carrie throughout the film, making Carrie carry the emotional burden of Margaret's religious guilt.

stimulus:
Sometimes the story you tell about yourself
doesn't match the evidence.

And someone you love
keeps asking you to watch the tape.

When that happens,
do you tend to insist on your version,
or let the picture speak?

And if you can't do either—
do you make them feel the shame too,
or carry it alone?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Tomas repeatedly refuses to admit he ran from the avalanche despite video evidence, insisting on 'his version' of events. When finally confronted with the footage, he deflects by remaining silent while Mats offers justifications. Later, his confession and breakdown in front of his children spreads his shame to them - they end up crying together. This is projective identification: he cannot contain his own shame, so he unconsciously evokes it in those around him (Ebba's anger, the children's distress, Mats and Fanny's fight).

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone who needs you
in a way that feels like devotion at first.

But then the devotion starts to look like you.
Your clothes. Your voice. Your life.

When someone mirrors you too closely,
do you feel flattered,
or do you feel erased?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Hedy progressively mimics Allie - copying her clothing, getting the same haircut and color, using her name at the sex club, and eventually trying to replace her entirely. This represents projective identification where Hedy projects her need for identity/fusion onto Allie and forces Allie to contain those unbearable feelings of emptiness and loss (from her twin's death). Allie initially accepts this (feeling flattered/motherly) but eventually feels invaded and erased.

stimulus:
Sometimes you meet someone new
and someone who already knows them.

You could keep those worlds separate.
Or you could let one inform the other—
listening to old complaints, using them as a map.

When you're getting to know someone,
do you prefer to form your own view,
or do you find yourself borrowing the opinions of those who came before?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Eva befriends Marianne without realizing she's Albert's ex-wife, then continues both relationships secretly. She actively encourages Marianne to voice complaints about Albert so she can 'identify potential problems' in her own relationship with him—projecting Marianne's negative feelings onto Albert and using them to sabotage her own relationship.

stimulus:
Sometimes you feel something painful
about someone close to you.

And instead of sitting with it alone,
you act in a way that makes them feel it too.

Not to hurt them—
but so they'll finally understand what it was like.

When you've been hurt,
do you tend to withdraw and process it privately,
or do you unconsciously pass the feeling outward
so the other person can know it from the inside?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Amy discovers Nick's affair and becomes resentful. Rather than confronting him directly or leaving, she elaborates an extreme plan to frame him for her murder—forcing him to experience fear, public humiliation, loss of control, and the threat of death. She doesn't just want revenge; she engineers a situation where Nick must inhabit the emotional reality she felt: betrayal, powerlessness, and being misunderstood by everyone. This is projective identification—evacuating her intolerable feelings into him and controlling him so he experiences what she couldn't bear alone.

stimulus:
Sometimes someone uses you
to feel something they can't admit they want.

They pull you close,
then remind you of the distance.

When that happens,
do you tend to notice it right away,
or do you keep playing the part they gave you?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Chunhyang makes love to Bangja multiple times due to his genuine feelings, yet seeks to marry Mong-ryong for social standing and enlists Bangja's help. Later, she and Mong-ryong orchestrate events that lead to Bangja's imprisonment without his knowledge. Chunhyang projects her own desires and internal conflict onto Bangja, using him to experience intimacy while maintaining her social ambitions, making him carry the emotional burden she cannot consciously integrate.

stimulus:
When someone hurts you,
there's a space that opens up—
between you and them, or you and the world.

Some people walk away from that space.
Others walk into it.

When you've been wronged,
do you pull back and protect what's left,
or do you move closer to the source?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Mrs. Mott, after losing everything due to Claire's accusation against her husband, does not retreat or distance herself. Instead, she infiltrates the Bartel family by posing as their nanny, inserting herself into the intimate core of their lives. This demonstrates projective identification—she externalizes her pain and rage by embedding herself within the target, making them unconsciously carry and enact her internal turmoil.
film: The Hand That Rocks the Cradle

stimulus:
Sometimes you see something wrong
and everyone around you acts like it's not there.

You can feel the pressure—
to stay quiet, to fit in, to not make it harder on yourself.

But the anger doesn't go away.
It just sits inside you, looking for a place to land.

When that happens,
do you tend to turn it inward,
or push it outward onto the world?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The students endure systemic abuse while the school, police, prosecutors, and community cover it up. In-ho and Yoo-jin face this wall of silence and complicity. Min-su, having lost hope after the light sentences, projects his despair and rage outward—stabbing the abuser and pulling both of them to their deaths on the railroad tracks. This is projective identification: the unbearable internal experience (trauma, powerlessness, rage) is externalized and enacted upon another, forcing them to feel what the victim felt.

stimulus:
There are people you admire
and people you resent.

Sometimes they're the same person.

When someone embodies what you can't be,
do you treat them as separate from you—
someone to follow or someone to fight?

Or do you eventually realize
they were never really outside you at all?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: The narrator creates Tyler Durden as a split personality—idealizing him as everything he wishes he could be (confident, free, rebellious) while remaining unaware they are the same person. This is splitting: dividing aspects of the self into 'all good' (Tyler) and 'all bad' (the narrator's mundane life) rather than integrating them into one complex identity.

stimulus:
Some people split the world cleanly in two:
the good ones and the dangerous ones.
The pure and the corrupting.
The self you protect, and the self that threatens it.

When someone new enters your life,
do you tend to see them in shades,
or do you sort them quickly into safe or unsafe?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Nina's splitting defense is evident throughout the film: she views herself as the pure, innocent White Swan while projecting all 'dark' qualities onto Lily (the Black Swan/rival). She cannot integrate both innocent and sensual aspects within herself, instead splitting them into all-good (Nina/Odette) versus all-bad (Lily/Odile). This rigid categorization extends to her relationship with her mother (all-protective vs. all-controlling) and intensifies as she descends into madness, unable to hold psychological complexity or ambiguity.

stimulus:
There are people who do terrible things.

And sometimes,
they become only that terrible thing in your mind.

No history. No reason. No complexity.
Just a shape you need to destroy.

When someone harms what you love,
do you try to understand them,
or does understanding feel like betrayal?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Jane witnesses her husband Mike being murdered by the Slasher and becomes obsessed with tracking him down. The Slasher is portrayed as purely evil (Satanic symbols, killing priests, drinking blood, trying to 'open gates of Hell'), reduced to a monstrous figure rather than a complex person. Jane's single-minded pursuit suggests she has split him into an all-bad object with no nuance, which is characteristic of the splitting defense mechanism.

stimulus:
Sometimes the person you love
turns into someone you can't recognize.

When that happens,
some people split them in two:
the one they loved, and the one who hurt them.
Two separate people.

Do you tend to hold both versions at once,
or does your mind need to separate them
to make sense of it?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: This comes from the film's core structure where Betty/Diane cannot reconcile her idealized love for Camilla with the betrayal and rejection she experiences. The narrative literally splits into two realities - the fantasy (first two acts) where Rita/Camilla is the vulnerable amnesiac Betty rescues and loves, versus the reality (final act) where Camilla is the successful actress who left her. Diane's mind splits Camilla into 'all good' (Rita, the helpless lover) and 'all bad' (Camilla, the betrayer), unable to hold the complexity of a real person who could both love and leave her.

stimulus:
There are people who fight loudly for what's right.
They hold signs. They lose their temper. They don't stop.

And sometimes,
they're called crazy for it.

When you see someone like that,
do you think:
they're brave and the system is broken,
or
they've lost perspective and become the problem?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Mannar Mannan is seen as a joker by others for his constant protests and lawsuits against injustices. He's dismissed, beaten by police, and eventually loses his mind while fighting the system. The village sees him as crazy, but his associates see him as fighting real wrongs. The plot embodies splitting: is he a heroic activist or a delusional troublemaker? The answer depends on which side you're viewing from.

stimulus:
Some people become everything you once thought they were.
Then they do one thing—
and suddenly they're unrecognizable.

In those moments,
do you tend to see them as two different people,
or try to hold both versions in your mind at once?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Amy's character demonstrates extreme splitting: she oscillates between viewing Nick as either perfect (rekindling attraction after his interview) or entirely villainous (framing him for murder). She cannot hold ambivalence—people are either idealized or demonized. Nick also experiences this when he discovers Amy is alive and has orchestrated everything, forcing him to reconcile the Amy he thought he knew with the Amy who exists. The defense mechanism of splitting is central to Amy's psychology and the audience's experience of her character transformation.

stimulus:
Some people stay complicated in your memory.
They weren't all bad. There were good moments.
But the harm was real too.

When someone has hurt you,
do you tend to hold both at once,
or does one version take over completely?

Why?
evidence_from_plot: Alex Forrest's character demonstrates splitting - she oscillates between clinging affection (spending the day with Dan, apologizing at his office) and extreme destructive behavior (cutting wrists, boiling the rabbit, kidnapping Ellen, attempting murder).
